questions.question: Explain the need for constructors in C++. What are the special characteristics of a constructor?
keyPoints: Constructors initialize objects when created
Name same as class name
No return type (not even void)
Automatically invoked on object creation
Can be overloaded
Usually declared in public section

questions.question: What is a copy constructor? When is it invoked? Give its general syntax.
keyPoints: Creates object as copy of another object of same class
Invoked when: object passed by value, returned by value, initialized from another object
Syntax: ClassName(const ClassName& obj)

questions.question: Explain the difference between default constructor and parameterized constructor with examples.
keyPoints: Default: no parameters or all defaults, e.g., MyClass() or MyClass(int a=5)
Parameterized: accepts arguments, e.g., MyClass(int x, int y)
Default constructor provided by compiler if none defined
Parameterized allows custom initialization

questions.question: What is a member initializer list? Why is it preferred over assignment in constructor body?
keyPoints: Syntax: Constructor() : member1(val1), member2(val2) {}
More efficient - initializes directly, no default then assign
Required for: const members, reference members, objects without default constructor
Reduces overhead of temporary objects

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'explicit' keyword with constructors? Give an example.
keyPoints: Prevents implicit conversions from single-parameter constructor
Use case: to avoid accidental type conversions
Example: explicit MyClass(int x) { value = x; }
Without explicit: MyClass obj = 10; //allowed, creates object from int
With explicit: MyClass obj = 10; //error, must use MyClass obj(10);

questions.question: Explain the order of constructor execution in single inheritance. How is the destructor order different?
keyPoints: Constructor: Base class first, then derived class
Destructor: Derived class first, then base class (reverse order)
Ensures proper initialization before use and cleanup after use

questions.question: What are the different types of constructors available in C++? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Default: No parameters
Parameterized: Accepts arguments
Copy: Creates copy of another object
Static: For static members (C++11+)
Explicit: Prevents implicit conversions
Private: For singleton pattern

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate constructor overloading with a class 'Complex' having real and imaginary parts. Include default, parameterized, and copy constructors. Also show usage from main().
keyPoints: Class Complex with real, imag data members
Default constructor initializes to 0,0
Parameterized constructor takes two arguments
Copy constructor creates copy of existing object
main() demonstrates all three types
Proper output display

questions.question: Explain what is a copy constructor. Write a C++ class 'String' with a parameterized constructor and a copy constructor. Also explain when copy constructor is called with examples.
keyPoints: Copy constructor definition and syntax
String class with char* for dynamic string
Parameterized constructor allocates memory
Copy constructor performs deep copy (not shallow)
Called in 4 scenarios with examples
Proper memory management (destructor)

questions.question: Explain the concept of member initializer list in C++. Write a class demonstrating its use. Why is it mandatory for const and reference members?
keyPoints: Syntax explanation with example
Class with const int, reference, and object members
Demonstrate initializer list usage
Const members must be initialized once, cannot be assigned
References must be bound at initialization
Objects of classes without default constructor must use initializer list
Output: Base Constructor then Derived Constructor
Then Derived Destructor then Base Destructor
Explain base-first, derived-next for constructors
Explain reverse order for destructors
Why: ensure proper initialization before use, proper cleanup after use
